text: In addition to their traditional use in petrochemistry, proton-exchanged zeolites are currently used in the petroleum industry as catalysts and tumbling catalysts for the conversion of hydrocarbons and oxygenates. Understanding the mechanisms of catalytic conversions on zeolites has been at the center of research activities for decades, and, with increasingly accurate kinetic studies, in situ and operando spectroscopy, the knowledge of how to induce a certain reaction on zeolites has evolved towards a union of the effect of acidity and the role of steric constraints, two concepts that have today become essential in the fields of heterogeneous catalysis and molecular chemistry. This review provides a molecular description of the activity of zeolites based on the fundamental concepts which are derived from catalytic kinetic studies as well as from in situ and operando spectroscopy, completed with quantum chemical calculations. After discussing the description of the Brønsted sites and their catalytic activity, the molecular mechanisms of the different types of catalytic conversion on zeolites (alkene conversions, alkane transformations, aromatic conversions, alcohol transformations and finally polyhydroxy compounds (sugars) are detailed. The elementary steps of the C–C, C–H and C–O bond formations and activations are described, and their corresponding catalytic sites are discussed. A perspective to achieve the future goals of zeolite catalysis is finally discussed, aiming towards the goal of predicting new Brønsted acid catalysts.
